one_liner: A 24-year-old male smoker presents with hemoptysis, rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, and bilateral alveolar infiltrates.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient presents to the ED with 5 days of worsening hemoptysis — initially blood-streaked sputum progressing to frank hemoptysis (estimated half cup per episode, 3-4 times daily). He also reports progressive dyspnea, fatigue, and dark tea-colored urine over the past week. He noticed bilateral ankle swelling 3 days ago. He denies fever, weight loss, joint pain, or skin rash. He smokes half a pack per day and was exposed to organic solvents (paint thinner) at his auto body repair job without respiratory protection 2 weeks before symptom onset.
stage2.pmh: No significant past medical history. No prior renal disease.
stage2.family_history: No family history of autoimmune disease or kidney disease.
stage2.social_history: Auto body repair worker with hydrocarbon solvent exposure. Current smoker (5 pack-years). Occasional alcohol. No IV drug use. Lives alone in an apartment.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Pale, ill-appearing young man in moderate respiratory distress**. HEENT: **Pale conjunctivae**. Lungs: **Diffuse bilateral crackles throughout all lung fields**; No wheezing. Cardiovascular: **Tachycardic, regular, S3 gallop**; **BP elevated**. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly. Extremities: **2+ bilateral pitting edema to ankles**; No rash or joint swelling. Skin: Pallor.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Diagnostic: anti-glomerular basement membrane antibodies directed against the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen are the hallmark of this condition
Markedly elevated; rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (baseline likely ~1.0)
Hyperkalemia from renal failure — cardiac monitoring needed
Severe anemia from pulmonary hemorrhage; likely also dilutional component from fluid overload

lab_results.result: 3+ blood, 2+ protein, >50 RBC/HPF, RBC casts present
lab_results.clinical_significance: Active glomerulonephritis: RBC casts are pathognomonic for glomerular disease
Nephrotic-range proteinuria indicating significant glomerular damage
Negative ANCA helps distinguish isolated this condition from 'double-positive' disease (anti-GBM + ANCA, seen in ~30% of cases)
Normal complement levels — this condition is not complement-mediated (unlike lupus nephritis)

imaging_results.result: Bilateral diffuse alveolar infiltrates in a perihilar and basilar distribution. No pleural effusions. Normal cardiac silhouette.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Bilateral alveolar opacities in the context of hemoptysis represent diffuse alveolar hemorrhage

imaging_results.result: Diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities and consolidations consistent with alveolar hemorrhage. No masses or cavitation. No pleural effusions.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage pattern; the ground-glass component represents intra-alveolar blood

imaging_results.result: Normal-sized kidneys bilaterally with increased cortical echogenicity. No hydronephrosis or masses.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Increased echogenicity suggests intrinsic renal disease (glomerulonephritis); normal size indicates acute rather than chronic process

stage5.final_clue: The patient is started on plasmapheresis, high-dose corticosteroids, and cyclophosphamide.

debrief.classic_presentation: Young male smoker with hydrocarbon exposure presenting with the pulmonary-renal syndrome: hemoptysis with diffuse alveolar hemorrhage AND rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (rising creatinine, RBC casts), with strongly positive anti-GBM antibodies and linear IgG on renal biopsy.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM disease) is a rare but life-threatening autoimmune disease. The key distinguishing feature from other pulmonary-renal syndromes (GPA, MPA, SLE) is the linear IgG pattern on immunofluorescence (vs. granular in immune complex disease, pauci-immune in ANCA vasculitis). Smoking and hydrocarbon exposure are triggers that damage the alveolar basement membrane, exposing the alpha-3(IV)NC1 antigen. Pulmonary hemorrhage occurs almost exclusively in smokers because the intact alveolar basement membrane normally prevents antibody access. ~30% of patients are 'double-positive' with both anti-GBM and ANCA — these patients may behave more like ANCA vasculitis with a relapsing course.
debrief.pathophysiology: Anti-GBM antibodies are directed against the non-collagenous (NC1) domain of the alpha-3 chain of type IV collagen, which is found in the glomerular and alveolar basement membranes. In the kidney, antibodies bind the GBM, activate complement, and trigger crescentic (rapidly progressive) glomerulonephritis. In the lung, alveolar capillary damage from smoking or inhalational exposures exposes the alveolar BM to circulating anti-GBM antibodies, causing diffuse alveolar hemorrhage. Linear IgG staining on immunofluorescence is the hallmark — this is a type II hypersensitivity reaction.
debrief.treatment_overview: Plasmapheresis (plasma exchange) is the cornerstone — removes circulating anti-GBM antibodies. Perform daily for 14 days or until anti-GBM titers are undetectable. High-dose IV corticosteroids (pulse methylprednisolone then oral prednisone taper). Cyclophosphamide for immunosuppression to prevent new antibody formation. Dialysis-dependent patients with 100% crescents on biopsy have very poor renal prognosis. Lung hemorrhage is more responsive to treatment than renal disease. Smoking cessation is mandatory. Renal transplant should be deferred until anti-GBM antibodies are negative for >=6 months.
high_yield_pearls: Linear IgG staining on immunofluorescence of renal biopsy is pathognomonic for anti-GBM disease — this distinguishes it from granular (immune complex) and pauci-immune (ANCA) patterns.
Pulmonary hemorrhage in anti-GBM disease occurs almost exclusively in smokers or those with inhalational injuries — the intact alveolar membrane normally protects against antibody deposition.
The target antigen is the NC1 domain of alpha-3(IV) collagen in the glomerular and alveolar basement membranes.
Plasmapheresis is the cornerstone of treatment — removes circulating pathogenic antibodies more rapidly than immunosuppression alone.
Goodpasture syndrome rarely relapses after successful treatment (unlike ANCA vasculitis), because the autoimmune response is monoclonal and self-limited.
sources: KDIGO 2021 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Glomerular Diseases — https://kdigo.org/guidelines/
McAdoo SP, Pusey CD. Anti-Glomerular Basement Membrane Disease. Clin J Am Soc Nephrol. 2017 — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/
UpToDate: Anti-GBM (Goodpasture) disease: Treatment and prognosis — https://www.uptodate.com/contents/search
